Percy Spencer discovered that microwaves could heat food when a chocolate bar in his pocket melted near a radar device. 36.passage: The ocean covers more than seventy percent of the Earth's surface and plays a crucial role in regulating our planet's climate. 37.passage: The story of Helen Keller is one of the most remarkable stories of human determination ever told. Struck by illness at nineteen months old, she became both deaf and blind. Her world was one of silence and darkness with no way to communicate. Then at age seven, her teacher Anne Sullivan arrived and began the patient work of reaching her. The breakthrough came when Anne held Helen's hand under running water and spelled 'water' into her palm. Helen suddenly understood that everything had a name — and a world opened up to her. She went on to learn multiple languages, graduate from college, write twelve books, and campaign for the rights of people with disabilities worldwide. Her life demonstrates that with the right support and an unbreakable will, barriers once thought impossible can be overcome.

37.passage: The concept of zero is so familiar today that it is hard to imagine a world without it. Yet zero did not exist in ancient Greek or Roman mathematics. 38.passage: Forests are among the most important ecosystems on Earth, yet they are disappearing at an alarming rate. 38.passage: Failure is something most people try to avoid, yet the greatest achievements in history have often come after repeated failures. Thomas Edison made thousands of unsuccessful attempts before inventing a working light bulb. J.K. Rowling's Harry Potter manuscript was rejected by twelve publishers before being accepted. 39.passage: The night sky has inspired humans since the beginning of time. Ancient civilizations studied the stars to tell the time, navigate the seas, and mark the seasons for planting crops. The Egyptians aligned their great pyramids with certain stars. Greek and Arab astronomers mapped the heavens and laid the foundations for modern astronomy. 39.passage: Kindness is sometimes seen as a soft or unimportant quality, but research increasingly shows that it is one of the most powerful forces in human society. Studies have found that acts of kindness release feel-good chemicals in the brains of both the giver and the receiver — and even in people who simply witness an act of kindness. Schools that teach empathy and cooperation alongside academic subjects report lower rates of bullying and higher student wellbeing. In business, leaders who show genuine kindness and care for their employees tend to build more loyal and productive teams. In communities, small regular acts of kindness — helping a neighbor, volunteering, or simply listening — build the social trust that holds societies together. Far from being weakness, kindness may be the quality that civilization depends on most.
